questions.question_text: Let $$ A = \{1, 2, 3\} $$ and consider the relation $$ R = \{(1, 2), (2, 1)\} $$. Then, the relation $$ R $$ is:
options: Reflexive but not symmetric
Symmetric but not transitive
Transitive but not symmetric
Equivalence relation

topper_solution.step_1: Check reflexivity: $$ (1, 1) \notin R $$, so $$ R $$ is not reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Check symmetry: $$ (1, 2) \in R $$ and $$ (2, 1) \in R $$, so $$ R $$ is symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Check transitivity: $$ (1, 2) \in R $$ and $$ (2, 1) \in R $$, but $$ (1, 1) \notin R $$. Hence, $$ R $$ is not transitive.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Provide a counterexample for reflexivity and transitivity to quickly score the mark without long explanations.
common_student_mistakes: Assuming that since (1, 2) and (2, 1) are present, it must be transitive.
Forgetting to check if (1, 1) exists for transitivity when aRb and bRa.
questions.concept_trigger: Presence of specific ordered pairs in a small finite set.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing basic definitions of reflexive, symmetric, and transitive relations using a simple counterexample.

questions.question_text: The function $$ f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $$ defined by $$ f(x) = 3 - 4x $$ is:
options: One-one but not onto
Onto but not one-one
Bijective (One-one and onto)
Neither one-one nor onto

topper_solution.step_1: For one-one: Let $$ f(x_1) = f(x_2) $$. Then $$ 3 - 4x_1 = 3 - 4x_2 $$, which gives $$ x_1 = x_2 $$. Thus, $$ f $$ is one-one.
topper_solution.step_2: For onto: Let $$ y \in \mathbb{R} $$ (codomain). Then $$ y = 3 - 4x $$ gives $$ x = \frac{3 - y}{4} $$.
topper_solution.step_3: Since for every real $$ y $$, $$ x = \frac{3 - y}{4} $$ is a real number in the domain, $$ f $$ is onto. Hence, it is bijective.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Always state the domain and codomain explicitly when expressing x in terms of y for surjectivity.
common_student_mistakes: Confusing the domain constraints, though here it is all real numbers.
Making sign errors when isolating x.
questions.concept_trigger: Linear function from real to real numbers.
questions.examiner_intent: Checking if students can formally prove injectivity and surjectivity for standard polynomial functions.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): The relation $$ R $$ in the set of real numbers $$ \mathbb{R} $$ defined as $$ R = \{(a, b) : a \le b^2\} $$ is not symmetric.\nReason (R): For real numbers, if $$ a \le b^2 $$, it does not necessarily imply that $$ b \le a^2 $$.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.correct_answer: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A

topper_solution.step_1: Analyze Assertion (A): Let $$ a = 1 $$ and $$ b = 4 $$. Then $$ 1 \le 4^2 $$ (i.e., $$ 1 \le 16 $$) is true, so $$ (1, 4) \in R $$.
topper_solution.step_2: Check symmetry for Assertion (A): But $$ 4 \le 1^2 $$ (i.e., $$ 4 \le 1 $$) is false. So $$ (4, 1) \notin R $$. Hence, A is true (it is not symmetric).
topper_solution.step_3: Analyze Reason (R): R correctly provides the mathematical reasoning and counter-logic for why symmetry fails. Thus, R is the correct explanation.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Using a numerical counterexample is the fastest and most accepted way to disprove a universal statement in CBSE.
common_student_mistakes: Thinking it is symmetric because it works for some numbers like (2, 2).
Misinterpreting 'not symmetric' as a false statement.
questions.concept_trigger: Inequality relations with powers usually fail symmetry and transitivity.
questions.examiner_intent: Evaluating analytical thinking and the ability to find counterexamples for abstract relations.

questions.question_text: Find the maximum number of equivalence relations on the set $$ A = \{1, 2, 3\} $$ containing the elements $$ (1, 2) $$ and $$ (2, 1) $$.

topper_solution.step_1: The smallest equivalence relation containing $$ (1, 2) $$ and $$ (2, 1) $$ must also be reflexive. So, $$ R_1 = \{(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (2, 1)\} $$.
topper_solution.step_2: The largest equivalence relation on set $$ A $$ is the universal relation $$ R_2 = A \times A $$, which contains all 9 ordered pairs.
topper_solution.step_3: Any relation adding just one more pair to $$ R_1 $$ forces the addition of others to maintain transitivity and symmetry, leading straight to $$ R_2 $$. Hence, exactly 2 relations exist.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Explicitly list $$ R_1 $$ and state that the universal relation is the only other possibility.
common_student_mistakes: Guessing the number without constructing the relations.
Forgetting to include the reflexive pairs (1,1), (2,2), (3,3).
questions.concept_trigger: Fixed set of elements and conditions for building a relation block by block.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing combinatorial understanding of equivalence relations on small finite sets.

questions.question_text: Show that the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ Z $$ of integers given by $$ R = \{(a, b) : 2 \text{ divides } (a - b)\} $$ is symmetric.

topper_solution.step_1: Let $$ (a, b) \in R $$. This means that $$ 2 \text{ divides } (a - b) $$.
topper_solution.step_2: Therefore, $$ a - b = 2k $$ for some integer $$ k $$.
topper_solution.step_3: Then, $$ b - a = -2k = 2(-k) $$. Since $$ -k $$ is also an integer, $$ 2 \text{ divides } (b - a) $$, meaning $$ (b, a) \in R $$. Hence, $$ R $$ is symmetric.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Write 'Let (a, b) ∈ R', introduce a constant 'k ∈ Z', and logically deduce '(b, a) ∈ R'.
common_student_mistakes: Explaining in words without using the algebraic '2k' representation.
Forgetting to specify that 'k' and '-k' belong to the set of integers.
questions.concept_trigger: Divisibility conditions in relations always require algebraic substitution.
questions.examiner_intent: Checking foundational algebraic proof writing skills for symmetric properties.

questions.question_text: Check the injectivity and surjectivity of the function $$ f: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} $$ given by $$ f(x) = x^2 $$.

topper_solution.step_1: Injectivity: Let $$ f(x_1) = f(x_2) $$. Then $$ x_1^2 = x_2^2 $$. Since $$ x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{N} $$, $$ x_1 = x_2 $$. Hence, $$ f $$ is one-one (injective).
topper_solution.step_2: Surjectivity: Let $$ y = 2 $$, where $$ 2 \in \mathbb{N} $$ (codomain).
topper_solution.step_3: Then $$ x^2 = 2 \implies x = \sqrt{2} $$, but $$ \sqrt{2} \notin \mathbb{N} $$ (domain). Since there is no natural number whose square is 2, $$ f $$ is not onto (not surjective).

questions.board_presentation_tip: Use a clear counterexample (like y=2) to quickly disprove surjectivity.
common_student_mistakes: Saying it's not one-one because (-2)^2 = (2)^2 without checking the domain (Natural numbers).
Failing to provide a specific counterexample for surjectivity.
questions.concept_trigger: Domain and codomain are Natural numbers, not Real numbers.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing attention to detail regarding the specified domain and codomain.

questions.question_text: Let $$ A = \mathbb{R} - \{3\} $$ and $$ B = \mathbb{R} - \{1\} $$. Consider the function $$ f: A \to B $$ defined by $$ f(x) = \frac{x - 2}{x - 3} $$. Show that $$ f $$ is one-one and onto.

topper_solution.step_1: One-one: Let $$ f(x_1) = f(x_2) $$. Then $$ \frac{x_1 - 2}{x_1 - 3} = \frac{x_2 - 2}{x_2 - 3} $$. Cross-multiplying gives $$ x_1x_2 - 3x_1 - 2x_2 + 6 = x_1x_2 - 2x_1 - 3x_2 + 6 $$, which simplifies to $$ -3x_1 - 2x_2 = -2x_1 - 3x_2 $$, leading to $$ x_1 = x_2 $$. Thus, $$ f $$ is one-one.
topper_solution.step_2: Onto: Let $$ y = f(x) $$. Then $$ y = \frac{x - 2}{x - 3} $$. Solving for $$ x $$, we get $$ xy - 3y = x - 2 \implies x(y - 1) = 3y - 2 \implies x = \frac{3y - 2}{y - 1} $$.
topper_solution.step_3: Since $$ y \in B $$, $$ y \neq 1 $$, so $$ x $$ is well-defined. Also, $$ x = 3 $$ would mean $$ 3y - 2 = 3y - 3 $$, which is impossible. Thus $$ x \in A $$. Since every $$ y \in B $$ has a pre-image in $$ A $$, $$ f $$ is onto.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Show the cross-multiplication expansion clearly for the one-one proof, and explicitly prove that x lies in domain A (i.e., x ≠ 3).
common_student_mistakes: Making algebraic sign errors during cross-multiplication.
Finding x in terms of y but forgetting to explicitly state that the denominator y-1 ≠ 0 because y ∈ R - {1}.
questions.concept_trigger: Rational function with excluded points in domain and codomain.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing algebraic manipulation and the formal definition of onto functions involving domain restrictions.

questions.question_text: Show that the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} $$ given by $$ R = \{(a, b) : |a - b| \text{ is even}\} $$ is an equivalence relation.

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexive: For any $$ a \in A $$, $$ |a - a| = 0 $$, which is an even number. Thus, $$ (a, a) \in R $$. So, $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetric: Let $$ (a, b) \in R $$. Then $$ |a - b| $$ is even. Since $$ |b - a| = |-(a - b)| = |a - b| $$, $$ |b - a| $$ is also even. Thus, $$ (b, a) \in R $$. So, $$ R $$ is symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitive: Let $$ (a, b) \in R $$ and $$ (b, c) \in R $$. This means $$ a - b = \pm 2k_1 $$ and $$ b - c = \pm 2k_2 $$. Adding these equations gives $$ a - c = \pm 2k_1 \pm 2k_2 = 2(\pm k_1 \pm k_2) $$. Since $$ a - c $$ is a multiple of 2, $$ |a - c| $$ is even. Thus $$ (a, c) \in R $$. R is an equivalence relation.

questions.board_presentation_tip: When proving transitivity for absolute values, drop the absolute value by using ± signs for rigorous proof.
common_student_mistakes: Stating |a - b| + |b - c| = |a - c|, which is mathematically incorrect (Triangle Inequality applies, not equality).
Forgetting to mention that 0 is an even integer for reflexivity.
questions.concept_trigger: Absolute value condition mapped to 'even' or 'divisible by' properties.
questions.examiner_intent: Evaluating the ability to formalize absolute value logic into discrete mathematical proofs.

questions.concept_tested: Complex Equivalence Relation on Cartesian Product
questions.question_text: Let $$ N $$ be the set of natural numbers and $$ R $$ be the relation on $$ N \times N $$ defined by $$ (a, b) R (c, d) $$ if $$ ad(b + c) = bc(a + d) $$. Show that $$ R $$ is an equivalence relation.
questions.correct_answer: Detailed proof of reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.

topper_solution.step_1: Rewrite condition: $$ ad(b+c) = bc(a+d) \implies \frac{b+c}{bc} = \frac{a+d}{ad} \implies \frac{1}{c} + \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{d} + \frac{1}{a} \implies \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{c} - \frac{1}{d} $$. (Note: Dividing by abcd is valid as a,b,c,d ∈ N).
topper_solution.step_2: Reflexive: For any $$ (a, b) \in N \times N $$, $$ \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{b} $$. Thus $$ (a, b) R (a, b) $$. $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_3: Symmetric: Let $$ (a, b) R (c, d) $$. Then $$ \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{c} - \frac{1}{d} \implies \frac{1}{c} - \frac{1}{d} = \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{b} $$. Thus $$ (c, d) R (a, b) $$. $$ R $$ is symmetric.\nTransitive: Let $$ (a, b) R (c, d) $$ and $$ (c, d) R (e, f) $$. Then $$ \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{c} - \frac{1}{d} $$ and $$ \frac{1}{c} - \frac{1}{d} = \frac{1}{e} - \frac{1}{f} $$. From these, $$ \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{e} - \frac{1}{f} $$, meaning $$ (a, b) R (e, f) $$. $$ R $$ is transitive. Hence, an equivalence relation.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Transforming the equation by dividing by abcd simplifies the entire proof. Show this transformation first before checking properties.
common_student_mistakes: Trying to prove transitivity directly by multiplying brackets, resulting in an unmanageable algebraic mess.
Forgetting that the relation elements are ordered pairs like (a, b) instead of single variables.
questions.concept_trigger: Cross-multiplied products involving sum of variables in Cartesian relations.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing high-level algebraic manipulation and the ability to simplify a relation before proving its properties.

questions.question_text:
Students of Class XII are participating in a school sports activity. Let $$ A = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\} $$ be the set of four selected students. A relation $$ R $$ on set $$ A $$ is defined as: two students are related if they have exactly the same height in centimeters.

Based on this, answer:
(i) Is the relation $$ R $$ reflexive?
(ii) If student $$ S_1 $$ is related to $$ S_2 $$, and $$ S_2 $$ is related to $$ S_3 $$, is $$ S_1 $$ related to $$ S_3 $$? Name this property.

topper_solution.step_1: For (i): Every student has the same height as themselves. So, height of $$ S_1 $$ = height of $$ S_1 $$. Thus $$ (S_1, S_1) \in R $$. The relation is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: For (ii): If $$ S_1 $$ is related to $$ S_2 $$, then height($$ S_1 $$) = height($$ S_2 $$). If $$ S_2 $$ is related to $$ S_3 $$, then height($$ S_2 $$) = height($$ S_3 $$).
topper_solution.step_3: By equality, height($$ S_1 $$) = height($$ S_3 $$), so $$ S_1 $$ is related to $$ S_3 $$. This property is called Transitivity.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Answer each sub-part clearly with a brief 1-line logical reason. Do not just write 'Yes'.
common_student_mistakes: Writing 'Yes' without providing the reasoning 'Every person has the same height as themselves'.
Confusing symmetric and transitive properties in word problems.
questions.concept_trigger: Real-world 'equality' conditions (same height, same book, same place) always form equivalence relations.
questions.examiner_intent: Connecting abstract mathematical relation concepts to practical, real-world situations.

options: Reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
Reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
Symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
An equivalence relation

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexive: $$ (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3) $$ are all present in $$ R $$. So, $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetric: $$ (1, 2) \in R $$ and $$ (2, 1) \in R $$. The other elements are doublets. So, $$ R $$ is symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitive: $$ (1, 2) \in R $$ and $$ (2, 1) \in R \implies (1, 1) \in R $$, which is present. $$ (2, 1) \in R $$ and $$ (1, 2) \in R \implies (2, 2) \in R $$, which is also present. So, $$ R $$ is transitive. Hence, it is an equivalence relation.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Check all three conditions systematically. For transitivity, check all pairs of the form (a,b) and (b,c).
common_student_mistakes: Missing the transitivity check for (2,1) and (1,2) leading to (2,2).
Confusing equivalence relation with universal relation.
questions.concept_trigger: Presence of all diagonal elements plus a symmetric pair usually indicates an equivalence relation.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing rigorous checking of equivalence relation conditions on a small set.

questions.question_text: The function $$ f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $$ defined by $$ f(x) = |x| $$ is:
options: One-one and onto
One-one but not onto
Onto but not one-one
Neither one-one nor onto

topper_solution.step_1: One-one check: Let $$ x_1 = 2 $$ and $$ x_2 = -2 $$. Both are in $$ \mathbb{R} $$. $$ f(2) = |2| = 2 $$ and $$ f(-2) = |-2| = 2 $$. Since $$ f(2) = f(-2) $$ but $$ 2 \neq -2 $$, $$ f $$ is not one-one.
topper_solution.step_2: Onto check: The codomain is $$ \mathbb{R} $$. Let $$ y = -1 $$. There is no real number $$ x $$ such that $$ |x| = -1 $$.
topper_solution.step_3: Since there are negative values in the codomain with no pre-image, $$ f $$ is not onto. Therefore, $$ f $$ is neither one-one nor onto.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Use counterexamples to disprove one-one and onto for absolute and even power functions.
common_student_mistakes: Thinking it is onto because every real number has a modulus.
Ignoring the codomain $$ \mathbb{R} $$ which includes negative numbers.
questions.concept_trigger: Modulus functions over real numbers are almost never one-one or onto.
questions.examiner_intent: Verifying understanding of the graphical and algebraic behavior of the absolute value function.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): The function $$ f: \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z} $$ defined by $$ f(x) = x + 2 $$ is a bijection.\nReason (R): Every element $$ y $$ in the codomain $$ \mathbb{Z} $$ has a unique pre-image $$ y - 2 $$ in the domain $$ \mathbb{Z} $$.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.correct_answer: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A

topper_solution.step_1: Analyze Assertion (A): For one-one, $$ f(x_1) = f(x_2) \implies x_1 + 2 = x_2 + 2 \implies x_1 = x_2 $$. For onto, $$ y = x + 2 \implies x = y - 2 $$. Since $$ y \in \mathbb{Z} $$, $$ x \in \mathbb{Z} $$. So, $$ f $$ is a bijection. A is true.
topper_solution.step_2: Analyze Reason (R): The reason correctly defines the onto condition and the uniqueness implicitly covers one-one. R is true.
topper_solution.step_3: Conclusion: Since R correctly explains why the function is bijective (both one-one and onto logic are satisfied), R is the correct explanation.

questions.board_presentation_tip: In Assertion-Reason questions, clearly verify the truth value of A and R separately before evaluating the linkage.
common_student_mistakes: Doubting surjectivity because of the domain $$ \mathbb{Z} $$ instead of $$ \mathbb{R} $$.
Failing to recognize that linear functions like $$ ax + b $$ are bijections if $$ a = \pm 1 $$ over integers.
questions.concept_trigger: Linear functions with coefficient 1 over integers are bijections.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing understanding of bijective definitions and their application to linear functions over discrete domains.

questions.question_text: Let $$ R $$ be an equivalence relation on the set $$ A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} $$ defined by $$ R = \{(a, b) : |a - b| \text{ is even}\} $$. Write the equivalence class of 1, denoted by $$ [1] $$.

topper_solution.step_1: The equivalence class of 1, denoted by $$ [1] $$, contains all elements $$ x \in A $$ such that $$ (x, 1) \in R $$.
topper_solution.step_2: This means $$ |x - 1| $$ must be an even number.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Explicitly define the equivalence class notation before writing the final set.
common_student_mistakes: Forgetting to include 1 in its own equivalence class.
Including negative numbers which are not in the domain set A.
questions.concept_trigger: Equivalence class asks for 'all elements related to the given element'.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing the concept of equivalence classes which partition the base set.

questions.concept_tested: Proving Not One-One and Not Onto
questions.question_text: Show that the function $$ f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $$ given by $$ f(x) = 1 + x^2 $$ is neither one-one nor onto.

topper_solution.step_1: Not One-One: Let $$ x_1 = 1 $$ and $$ x_2 = -1 $$. Both belong to the domain $$ \mathbb{R} $$.
topper_solution.step_2: Then $$ f(1) = 1 + (1)^2 = 2 $$ and $$ f(-1) = 1 + (-1)^2 = 2 $$. Since $$ f(1) = f(-1) $$ but $$ 1 \neq -1 $$, the function is not one-one.
topper_solution.step_3: Not Onto: Let $$ y = -2 $$ (an element in the codomain $$ \mathbb{R} $$). We need to find $$ x $$ such that $$ 1 + x^2 = -2 $$, which gives $$ x^2 = -3 $$. Since the square of a real number cannot be negative, there is no $$ x \in \mathbb{R} $$. Hence, $$ f $$ is not onto.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Always use specific, simple numerical counterexamples for disproving one-one and onto properties to save time and ensure clarity.
common_student_mistakes: Attempting a generalized algebraic proof when disproving, which often gets confusing.
Choosing a positive value for y (like y=5) which actually has a pre-image, failing to disprove surjectivity.
questions.concept_trigger: Even powers of x restrict the range to positive numbers, making it easy to disprove onto and one-one.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing ability to identify properties of quadratic functions over real numbers using counterexamples.

questions.question_text: Show that the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ A $$ of all polygons defined as $$ R = \{(P_1, P_2) : P_1 \text{ and } P_2 \text{ have the same number of sides}\} $$, is an equivalence relation.
questions.correct_answer: Proof of reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexive: For any polygon $$ P $$, it has the same number of sides as itself. So, $$ (P, P) \in R $$. Thus, $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetric: Let $$ (P_1, P_2) \in R $$. This means $$ P_1 $$ and $$ P_2 $$ have the same number of sides. This implies $$ P_2 $$ and $$ P_1 $$ have the same number of sides. Thus $$ (P_2, P_1) \in R $$. So, $$ R $$ is symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitive: Let $$ (P_1, P_2) \in R $$ and $$ (P_2, P_3) \in R $$. Then $$ P_1 $$ has same sides as $$ P_2 $$, and $$ P_2 $$ has same sides as $$ P_3 $$. Therefore, $$ P_1 $$ has the same number of sides as $$ P_3 $$, meaning $$ (P_1, P_3) \in R $$. Hence, $$ R $$ is an equivalence relation.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Keep the language simple and directly use the defining phrase 'same number of sides' in each step.
common_student_mistakes: Overcomplicating the proof by introducing variables for the number of sides without clearly linking them back to the polygons.
questions.concept_trigger: Relations based on 'sameness' (same sides, same book pages) are always equivalence relations.
questions.examiner_intent: Assessing application of relation properties to non-numerical sets.

questions.question_text: Show that the function $$ f: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} $$ defined by $$ f(n) = n+1 $$ if $$ n $$ is odd, and $$ f(n) = n-1 $$ if $$ n $$ is even, is both one-one and onto.

topper_solution.step_1: One-one: Let $$ f(n_1) = f(n_2) $$. Case I: Both are odd. $$ n_1+1 = n_2+1 \implies n_1=n_2 $$. Case II: Both are even. $$ n_1-1 = n_2-1 \implies n_1=n_2 $$. Case III: One odd, one even. Then $$ n_1+1 $$ (even) $$ = n_2-1 $$ (odd), which is impossible. Thus $$ f $$ is one-one.
topper_solution.step_2: Onto: Let $$ y \in \mathbb{N} $$ (codomain). If $$ y $$ is odd, let $$ x = y+1 $$. Since $$ y $$ is odd, $$ x $$ is even. Thus $$ f(x) = x-1 = (y+1)-1 = y $$.
topper_solution.step_3: If $$ y $$ is even, let $$ x = y-1 $$. Since $$ y $$ is even, $$ x $$ is odd. Thus $$ f(x) = x+1 = (y-1)+1 = y $$. Since every $$ y $$ has a pre-image in $$ \mathbb{N} $$, $$ f $$ is onto. Hence it is a bijection.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Address the three cases (odd-odd, even-even, odd-even) explicitly for injectivity to get full marks.
common_student_mistakes: Failing to consider the case where n1 is odd and n2 is even in the one-one proof.
Incorrectly defining the pre-image x based on the parity of y in the onto proof.
questions.concept_trigger: Piecewise functions mapping N to N usually swap consecutive integers, preserving bijection.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing logical division into cases for proving bijection in piecewise functions.

questions.question_text: Show that the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ A $$ of points in a plane given by $$ R = \{(P, Q) : \text{distance of point } P \text{ from origin is same as distance of } Q \text{ from origin}\} $$, is an equivalence relation. Further, show that the set of all points related to a point $$ P \neq (0, 0) $$ is the circle passing through $$ P $$ with origin as center.
questions.correct_answer: Proof of equivalence and circular locus.

topper_solution.step_1: Let $$ O $$ be the origin. Then $$ R = \{(P, Q) : OP = OQ\} $$. Reflexive: $$ OP = OP $$, so $$ (P, P) \in R $$. Symmetric: If $$ (P, Q) \in R \implies OP = OQ \implies OQ = OP \implies (Q, P) \in R $$.
topper_solution.step_2: Transitive: If $$ (P, Q) \in R $$ and $$ (Q, S) \in R $$, then $$ OP = OQ $$ and $$ OQ = OS $$. Thus $$ OP = OS $$, meaning $$ (P, S) \in R $$. So, $$ R $$ is an equivalence relation.
topper_solution.step_3: Locus part: The set of all points related to $$ P $$ is the set of points $$ Q $$ such that $$ OQ = OP $$ (a constant distance). The locus of points at a constant distance from a fixed point (origin) is a circle. Hence proved.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Use mathematical notation like 'OP = OQ' instead of writing long sentences about distances to keep the proof clean.
common_student_mistakes: Proving the equivalence relation but skipping the second part about the circle locus, losing 1 mark.
Not mentioning 'origin as center' when describing the circle.
questions.concept_trigger: Distance from a fixed point is the fundamental definition of a circle.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing equivalence relations combined with basic coordinate geometry concepts.

questions.question_text: Let $$ A = \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N} $$ and $$ R $$ be the relation on $$ A $$ defined by $$ (a, b) R (c, d) $$ if and only if $$ a + d = b + c $$. Prove that $$ R $$ is an equivalence relation.
questions.correct_answer: Detailed proof of reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexivity: Let $$ (a, b) \in \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N} $$. Since addition is commutative, $$ a + b = b + a $$. Therefore, $$ (a, b) R (a, b) $$. $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetry: Let $$ (a, b) R (c, d) $$. This implies $$ a + d = b + c $$. We can rewrite this as $$ c + b = d + a $$, which means $$ (c, d) R (a, b) $$. $$ R $$ is symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitivity: Let $$ (a, b) R (c, d) $$ and $$ (c, d) R (e, f) $$. Then $$ a + d = b + c $$ and $$ c + f = d + e $$. Adding these two equations gives $$ a + d + c + f = b + c + d + e $$.
topper_solution.step_4: Canceling $$ c $$ and $$ d $$ from both sides gives $$ a + f = b + e $$, which means $$ (a, b) R (e, f) $$. Thus, $$ R $$ is transitive. Since $$ R $$ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, it is an equivalence relation.

questions.board_presentation_tip: In transitivity, explicitly write out the addition of the two equations. Showing the cancellation step clearly earns full marks.
common_student_mistakes: Confusing the cross-addition rule $$ a+d = b+c $$ with direct addition $$ a+b = c+d $$.
Not clearly separating the four variables when proving transitivity.
questions.concept_trigger: Adding equations to eliminate middle variables is the standard method for Cartesian product relations.
questions.examiner_intent: Evaluating the ability to apply equivalence relation definitions to pairs of numbers instead of single elements.

questions.concept_tested: Equivalence Class in Real Life Scenario
questions.question_text:
In a school library, books are organized systematically. Let $$ A $$ be the set of all books in the library. A relation $$ R $$ is defined on $$ A $$ as $$ R = \{(x, y) : x 	ext{ and } y 	ext{ have the same number of pages}\} $$.

Based on this information, answer the following:
(i) Verify if $$ R $$ is an equivalence relation.
(ii) If book $$ x $$ is a Maths NCERT with 350 pages, what does the equivalence class of $$ x $$, denoted by $$ [x] $$, represent?
questions.correct_answer: (i) Yes, it is an equivalence relation. (ii) All books in the library with exactly 350 pages.

topper_solution.step_1: For (i): Reflexive: Every book has the same number of pages as itself. So $$ (x, x) \in R $$. Symmetric: If book $$ x $$ and $$ y $$ have the same pages, then $$ y $$ and $$ x $$ have the same pages. So $$ (x, y) \in R \implies (y, x) \in R $$.
topper_solution.step_2: Transitive: If $$ x $$ has same pages as $$ y $$, and $$ y $$ has same pages as $$ z $$, then $$ x $$ has same pages as $$ z $$. Hence, $$ R $$ is an equivalence relation.
topper_solution.step_3: For (ii): The equivalence class $$ [x] $$ contains all elements related to $$ x $$. Since $$ x $$ has 350 pages, $$ [x] $$ represents the set of all books in the library that contain exactly 350 pages.

questions.board_presentation_tip: For real-life context questions, use the scenario context (like 'number of pages') in your justification rather than purely abstract symbols.
common_student_mistakes: Defining the equivalence class mathematically without translating it back to the real-world meaning requested by the question.
questions.concept_trigger: Equivalence classes group items that share the defining property of the relation.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing comprehension of equivalence relations and classes in an applied, competency-based format.

questions.question_text: Let $$ A = \{x \in \mathbb{Z} : 0 \le x \le 12\} $$. Show that the relation $$ R $$ on $$ A $$ given by $$ R = \{(a, b) : |a - b| \text{ is a multiple of } 4\} $$ is an equivalence relation. Find the set of all elements related to 1. Also write the equivalence class $$ [2] $$.
questions.correct_answer: Proof of equivalence relation, related to 1: {1, 5, 9}, [2]: {2, 6, 10}

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexive: For any $$ a \in A $$, $$ |a - a| = 0 $$, which is a multiple of 4 (since $$ 0 = 4 \times 0 $$). Thus, $$ (a, a) \in R $$. $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetric: Let $$ (a, b) \in R $$. Then $$ |a - b| = 4k $$ for some integer $$ k $$. Since $$ |b - a| = |-(a - b)| = |a - b| = 4k $$, $$ (b, a) \in R $$. $$ R $$ is symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitive: Let $$ (a, b) \in R $$ and $$ (b, c) \in R $$. Then $$ a - b = \pm 4k_1 $$ and $$ b - c = \pm 4k_2 $$. Adding them, $$ a - c = 4(\pm k_1 \pm k_2) $$, which is a multiple of 4. So $$ |a - c| $$ is a multiple of 4. Thus $$ (a, c) \in R $$. $$ R $$ is an equivalence relation.
topper_solution.step_4: Elements related to 1: Let $$ x $$ be related to 1. Then $$ |x - 1| = 4k $$. For $$ k=0, 1, 2 $$, $$ x = 1, 5, 9 $$. So the set is $$ \{1, 5, 9\} $$. Equivalence class $$ [2] = \{x \in A : |x - 2| = 4k\} = \{2, 6, 10\} $$.

questions.board_presentation_tip: When adding terms for transitivity, always remove the absolute value by using plus-minus signs to ensure rigorous mathematical proof.
common_student_mistakes: Forgetting to limit the equivalence class elements to the set A (0 to 12).
Assuming |a - b| + |b - c| = |a - c| directly without removing mod signs.
questions.concept_trigger: Multiple of a number in an absolute value difference always forms an equivalence relation.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing formal equivalence relation proof and ability to compute specific equivalence classes under domain constraints.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): The function $$ f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $$ defined by $$ f(x) = e^x $$ is not an onto function.\nReason (R): The range of the exponential function $$ f(x) = e^x $$ is $$ (0, \infty) $$, which is a proper subset of the codomain $$ \mathbb{R} $$.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.correct_answer: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A

topper_solution.step_1: Analyze Assertion (A): For an onto function, the range must equal the codomain. The exponential function $$ e^x $$ only produces strictly positive real numbers. So, negative numbers and zero in the codomain $$ \mathbb{R} $$ have no pre-image. Thus, $$ f $$ is not onto. Assertion A is true.
topper_solution.step_2: Analyze Reason (R): The reason states the range is $$ (0, \infty) $$. Since $$ (0, \infty) \neq \mathbb{R} $$, this is exactly why it fails to be onto. Reason R is true and correctly explains A.

questions.board_presentation_tip: For onto/surjective questions, comparing Range and Codomain is the standard formal method.
common_student_mistakes: Assuming that since the domain is all real numbers, the range must also be all real numbers.
questions.concept_trigger: Exponential functions never yield negative values or zero.
questions.examiner_intent: Evaluating conceptual clarity on the definitions of range, codomain, and surjectivity.

questions.question_text: Check whether the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ \mathbb{R} $$ of real numbers defined by $$ R = \{(a, b) : a \le b^3\} $$ is reflexive, symmetric, or transitive.
questions.correct_answer: Neither reflexive, nor symmetric, nor transitive.

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexive: Let $$ a = \frac{1}{2} $$. Then $$ \frac{1}{2} \le \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 \implies \frac{1}{2} \le \frac{1}{8} $$, which is false. Thus, $$ \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right) \notin R $$. So, $$ R $$ is not reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetric: Let $$ a = 1, b = 2 $$. $$ 1 \le 2^3 \implies 1 \le 8 $$, which is true. But for $$ (b, a) $$, $$ 2 \le 1^3 \implies 2 \le 1 $$, which is false. Thus, $$ R $$ is not symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitive: Let $$ a = 10, b = 3, c = 1.5 $$. $$ (10, 3) \in R $$ because $$ 10 \le 27 $$. $$ (3, 1.5) \in R $$ because $$ 3 \le 3.375 $$. But $$ (10, 1.5) \notin R $$ because $$ 10 \le 3.375 $$ is false. Thus, $$ R $$ is not transitive.

questions.board_presentation_tip: For relations involving squares or cubes, fractions between 0 and 1 are the best counterexamples for reflexivity.
common_student_mistakes: Testing reflexivity with integers (like 2 <= 8), missing the fact that fractions fail the condition.
Failing to find a counterexample for transitivity, which requires carefully chosen numbers.
questions.concept_trigger: Higher powers with inequality over Real numbers usually fail all three relation properties.
questions.examiner_intent: Testing analytical thinking and ability to discover non-trivial counterexamples in real numbers.

questions.question_text: Show that the function $$ f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $$ given by $$ f(x) = x^3 + 1 $$ is a bijection.

topper_solution.step_1: One-one: Let $$ f(x_1) = f(x_2) $$. Then $$ x_1^3 + 1 = x_2^3 + 1 \implies x_1^3 = x_2^3 $$. Taking the cube root on both sides (which is unique in $$ \mathbb{R} $$), we get $$ x_1 = x_2 $$. Thus, $$ f $$ is one-one.
topper_solution.step_2: Onto: Let $$ y \in \mathbb{R} $$ (codomain). Then $$ y = x^3 + 1 \implies x^3 = y - 1 \implies x = (y - 1)^{1/3} $$.
topper_solution.step_3: Since the cube root of any real number is a real number, $$ x \in \mathbb{R} $$ for all $$ y \in \mathbb{R} $$. Thus, every element in the codomain has a pre-image, making $$ f $$ onto. Hence, it is a bijection.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Explicitly state that taking the cube root yields a unique real number. (Unlike square roots, which yield ± and cause non-injectivity).
common_student_mistakes: Confusing odd powers with even powers and falsely claiming it is not one-one.
Not clearly writing the pre-image x in terms of y.
questions.concept_trigger: Odd degree polynomials over Reals without turning points are always bijections.
questions.examiner_intent: Checking familiarity with algebraic properties of odd integer powers.

questions.question_text:
An IT company assigns a unique Employee ID (a Natural Number) to each of its newly hired software engineers. Let $$ A $$ be the set of all newly hired engineers and $$ \mathbb{N} $$ be the set of natural numbers. The assignment is modeled by a function $$ f: A 	o \mathbb{N} $$ where $$ f(e) $$ is the Employee ID assigned to engineer $$ e $$.

(i) Is the function $$ f $$ one-one (injective)? Justify your answer.
(ii) Is the function $$ f $$ onto (surjective)? Justify your answer.

topper_solution.step_1: For (i): The problem states each engineer gets a 'unique' Employee ID. This means no two different engineers get the same ID. Mathematically, if $$ e_1 \neq e_2 $$, then $$ f(e_1) \neq f(e_2) $$. Therefore, the function $$ f $$ is one-one.
topper_solution.step_2: For (ii): The codomain is $$ \mathbb{N} $$, which contains infinite natural numbers. However, the company has a finite number of newly hired engineers.
topper_solution.step_3: Thus, there will be many natural numbers (IDs) that are not assigned to anyone (e.g., ID 1000000 if there are only 50 hires). Since there are elements in the codomain without a pre-image, $$ f $$ is not onto.

questions.board_presentation_tip: Always link the mathematical definition back to the words used in the real-world prompt (e.g., 'unique', 'finite hires').
common_student_mistakes: Stating it is onto because every engineer gets an ID (confusing the definition of a function mapping with surjectivity).
Ignoring the domain and codomain sizes.
questions.concept_trigger: Mapping a finite set of people to an infinite set of numbers guarantees it cannot be onto.
questions.examiner_intent: Assessing application of injective and surjective definitions to practical logic scenarios.
